star.story.the_fool:
Atop the mist-shrouded train, there stands a figure holding a tattered deck of tarot cards, calling themselves "The Fool."

No one knows their true name. No one has seen the face behind the mask. They always stand in the deepest corner of the carriage, eyes peering through narrow slits, watching every passing traveler.

"Do you believe in fate?" they ask.

Most laugh and shake their heads. But some stop, drawn by those unfathomable eyes.

"Fate can be changed," The Fool whispers, "but first you must see its shape."

Then they offer a note — golden, covered in symbols no one can read. They call it the "Honored Note," a key to what lies above the mist.

Those who accept the note and pray become members of the "Tarot Assembly." When summoned, their souls leave their bodies and gather in a realm beyond reality. There, they vote to identify the one marked by fate — the "Heretic."

And The Fool's ancient pistol fires only at the Heretic.


"I am no judge," says The Fool. "I am merely fate's executor."

"The journey is not yet over."
star.story.role.mortician:
He was a night attendant at a funeral parlor, where he learned to deal with death. Silent remains, cold metal, delicate makeup — death for him was not an end, but a ceremony that needed to be completed with dignity.

The chaos on this train made him uncomfortable. People died without dignity, bodies discarded like forgotten garbage.

"At least," he thought, "I can let them pass away with dignity."

His eyes are somewhat special, able to see the scattered "relics" in darkness — those items dropped from the dead, carrying their final memories. 10 block range, y-axis 3 blocks, like his standard working distance.

And his true skill is "looting" — not greedy plunder, but a ritual-like behavior. He approaches corpses, opens their "second inventory," and selects one or two valuable items from the relics.

Is this sacrilege or respect? He himself couldn't say.

But one thing is certain: he cannot touch those "commands" — the power symbols woven by command blocks that control life and death. He didn't come to become a new controller, he just came to send them off.

For each corpse, he can only act once. This is the rule, and also his final respect for the dead.

"Rest in peace," he would whisper. "Please rest."
